name: synr
documentation:
# Synr

Synr is a library that provides a stable Abstract Syntax Tree for Python.

## Features

- The Synr AST does not change between Python versions.
- Every AST node contains line and column information.
- There is a single AST node for assignments (compared to three in Python's ast module).
- Support for returning multiple errors at once.
- Support for custom error reporting.

## Usage

```python
import synr

def test_program(x: int):
  return x + 2

# Parse a Python function into an AST
ast = synr.to_ast(test_program, synr.PrinterDiagnosticContext())
```

## Documentation

Please see [https://synr.readthedocs.io/en/latest/](https://synr.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) for documentation.

modules.name: synr
documentation:
The goal of synr is to provide a syntactic representation
for the core of the Python programming language for building
embedded domain specific langauges.

Synr provides
- A python version agnostic syntax tree.
- Line and column information for every node in the AST.

modules.name: synr.transformer
documentation:
This module handles converting a synr AST into the users desired
representation. We provide a visitor class that the user can inherit from to
write their conversion.

classes.name: synr.transformer.Transformer

methods.name: synr.transformer.Transformer.do_transform
documentation:
Entry point for the transformation.

This is called with the synr AST and the diagnostic context used in parsing the python AST.

methods.name: synr.transformer.Transformer.error
documentation: Report an error on a given span.

methods.name: synr.transformer.Transformer.transform
documentation:
Visitor function.

Call this to recurse into child nodes.
A visitor to handle user specified transformations on the AST.

modules.name: synr.diagnostic_context
documentation:
Error handling in Synr is done through a `DiagnosticContext`. This context
accumulates errors and renders them together after parsing has finished.

methods.name: synr.diagnostic_context.DiagnosticContext.add_source
documentation:
Add a file with source code to the context. This will be called
before any call to {py:func}`emit` that contains a span in this
file.

methods.name: synr.diagnostic_context.DiagnosticContext.emit
documentation: Called when an error has occured.

methods.name: synr.diagnostic_context.DiagnosticContext.render
documentation:
Render out all error messages. Can either return a value or raise
and execption.

classes.name: synr.diagnostic_context.PrinterDiagnosticContext
documentation:
Simple diagnostic context that prints the error and underlines its
location in the source. Raises a RuntimeError on the first error hit.

modules.name: synr.compiler
documentation: This module contains the main compiler from the python AST to the synr AST.

functions.name: synr.compiler._get_full_source
documentation:
Get full source code of the program

Parameters
----------
program : Any
    The python function or class.

source : str
    The source code of the program without other codes in the same file.

Returns
-------
str
    The full source code

functions.name: synr.compiler.to_ast
documentation:
Parse an abstract syntax tree from a Python program.

# Examples

{py:func}`to_ast` can be used with a given python function or class:

```python
import synr

def my_function(x):
    return x + 2

synr.to_ast(my_function, synr.PrinterDiagnosticContext())
```

{py:func}`to_ast` can also be used with a string containing python code:

```python
import synr
f = "def my_function(x):\\n    return x + 2"
synr.to_ast(f, synr.PrinterDiagnosticContext())
```

# Parameters

program

: A string containing a python program or a python function or class. If
  a python function or class is used, then line numbers will be localized
  to the file that the function or class came from.

diagnostic_ctx

: A diagnostic context to handle reporting of errors.

transformer

: An optional transformer to apply to the AST after parsing. The
  transformer allows for converting from synr's AST to a user specified
  AST using the same diagnostic context and error handling.

# Returns

Union[synr.ast.Module, Any]

: A synr AST if no transformer was specified and not errors occured. Or
  an error if one occured. Or the result of applying the transformer to
  the synr AST.

modules.name: synr.ast
documentation:
`synr.ast` contains definitions of all nodes in the synr AST. This AST is
independent of python version: different versions of python will give the same
AST. In addition, all nodes contain span information.

variables.name: synr.ast.Name
documentation: TODO: add span to this

classes.name: synr.ast.Span

methods.name: synr.ast.Span.from_ast
documentation: Extract the span of a python AST node

methods.name: synr.ast.Span.merge
documentation:
Return the span starting from the beginning of the first span and
ending at the end of the second span.

Notes
-----
Gaps between the end of the first span and the start of the second span
are contained in the merged span. The spans also do not need to be in
order.

Example
-------

    >>> Span("", 1, 2, 1, 4).merge(Span("", 2, 1, 2, 3))
    Span(filename="", start_line=1, start_column=2, end_line=2, end_column=3)
    >>> Span("", 2, 3, 2, 1).merge(Span("", 1, 2, 1, 4))
    Span(filename="", start_line=1, start_column=2, end_line=2, end_column=3)

methods.name: synr.ast.Span.union
documentation:
A span containing all the given spans with no gaps.

This function is the equivalent to merge with more than two spans.

Example
-------

    >>> Span.union(Span("", 1, 2, 1, 4), Span("", 2, 1, 2, 3))
    Span(filename="", start_line=1, start_column=2, end_line=2, end_column=3)

methods.name: synr.ast.Span.between
documentation:
The span starting after the end of the first span and ending before
the start of the second span.

Example
--------

    >>> Span("", 1, 2, 1, 4).between(Span("", 2, 1, 2, 3))
    Span(filename="", start_line=1, start_column=4, end_line=2, end_column=1)

methods.name: synr.ast.Span.subtract
documentation:
The span from the start of the first span to the start of the second span.

Example
--------

    >>> Span("", 1, 2, 2, 4).subtract(Span("", 2, 1, 2, 3))
    Span(filename="", start_line=1, start_column=2, end_line=2, end_column=1)

methods.name: synr.ast.Span.invalid
documentation: An invalid span

An contiguous interval in a source file from a starting line and column
to an ending line and column.

Notes
-----
Line and column numbers are one indexed. The interval spanned is inclusive
of the start and exclusive of the end.

classes.name: synr.ast.Node
documentation:
Base class of any AST node.

All AST nodes must have a span.

classes.name: synr.ast.Type
documentation: Base class of a type in the AST.

classes.name: synr.ast.Stmt
documentation: Base class of a statement in the AST.

classes.name: synr.ast.Expr
documentation: Base class of a expression in the AST.

classes.name: synr.ast.Parameter
documentation:
Parameter in a function declaration.

# Example

{code}`x: str` in {code}`def my_function(x: str)`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Var
documentation:
A variable in an expression.

# Examples

{code}`x` and {code}`y` in {code}`x = y`.
{code}`x` in {code}`x + 2`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Attr
documentation:
Field access on variable or structure or module.

# Examples

In {code}`x.y`, {code}`x` is the {code}`object` and {code}`y` is the
{code}`field`. For multiple field accesses in a row, they are grouped left
to right. For example, {code}`x.y.z` becomes
{code}`Attr(Attr(object='x', field='y'), field='z')`.

classes.name: synr.ast.TypeVar
documentation:
Type variable in a type expression.

This is equivalent to {code}`Var`, but for types.

# Example

{code}`X` in {code}`y: X = 2`.

classes.name: synr.ast.TypeAttr
documentation:
Field access in a type expression.

This is equivalent to {code}`Attr`, but for types.

# Example

In {code}`y: X.Z = 2`, {code}`object` is {code}`X` and {code}`field` is
{code}`Z`.

classes.name: synr.ast.BuiltinOp

class_variables.name: synr.ast.BuiltinOp.Invalid
documentation: placeholder op if op failed to parse

Various built in operators including binary operators, unary operators,
and array access.

# Examples

{code}`+` in {code}`2 + 3`.
{code}`[]` in {code}`x[2]`.
{code}`or` in {code}`true or false`.
{code}`>` in {code}`3 > 2`.

classes.name: synr.ast.TypeCall
documentation:
A function call in type expression.

This is equivalent to {code}`Call`, but for type expressions.

# Example

In {code}`x : List[type(None)]`, {code}`type(None)` is a {code}`TypeCall`.
{code}`type` is the {code}`func_name` and {code}`None` is
{code}`params[0]`.

classes.name: synr.ast.TypeApply
documentation:
Type application.

# Example

In {code}`x: List[str]`, {code}`List[str]` is a {code}`TypeCall`. In this
case, {code}`List` is the {code}`func_name`, and {code}`str` is
{code}`params[0]`.

classes.name: synr.ast.TypeTuple
documentation:
A tuple in a type expression.

# Example

{code}`(str, int)` in {code}`x: (str, int)`.

classes.name: synr.ast.TypeConstant
documentation:
A literal value in a type expression.

This is equivalent to {code}`Constant`, but for type expressions.

# Examples

{code}`1` in {code}`x: Array[1]`.
{code}`None` in {code}`def my_function() -> None:`.

classes.name: synr.ast.TypeSlice
documentation:
A slice in a type expression.

This is equivalent to {code}`Slice`, but for type expressions.

# Example

{code}`1:2` in {code}`x: Array[1:2]`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Tuple
documentation:
A tuple in an expression.

# Example

{code}`(1, 2)` in {code}`x = (1, 2)`.

classes.name: synr.ast.DictLiteral
documentation:
A sictionary literal in an expression.

# Example

{code}`{"x": 2}` in {code}`x = {"x": 2}`.

classes.name: synr.ast.ArrayLiteral
documentation:
An array literal in an expression.

# Example

{code}`[1, 2]` in {code}`x = [1, 2]`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Op
documentation:
A builtin operator.

See {code}`BuiltinOp` for supported operators.

# Example

{code}`+` in {code}`x = 1 + 2`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Constant
documentation:
A literal value in an expression.

# Example

{code}`1` in {code}`x = 1`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Call
documentation:
A function call.

Function calls can be:

- A regular function call of the form {code}`x.y(1, 2, z=3)`. In this case,
  {code}`func_name` will contain the function name (`x.y`), {code}`params`
  will contain the arguments (`1, 2`), and {code}`keywords_params` will
  contain any keyword arguments (`z=3`).
- A binary operation like {code}`x + 2`. In this case, {code}`func_name`
  will be the binary operation from {code}`BuiltinOp`, {code}`params[0]`
  will be the left hand side (`x`), and {code}`params[1]` will be the right
  hand side {code}`2`.
- A unary operation like {code}`not x`. In this case, {code}`func_name`
  will be the unary operation from {code}`BuiltinOp`, and {code}`params[0]`
  will be the operand (`x`).
- An array access like {code}`x[2, y]`. In this case, {code}`func_name`
  will be {code}`BuiltinOp.Subscript`, {code}`params[0]` will be the
  operand (`x`), {code}`params[1]` will be a {code}`Tuple` containing the
  indices (`2, y`).
- An array assignment like {code}`x[2, 3] = y`. In this case,
  {code}`func_name` will be {code}`BuiltinOp.SubscriptAssign`,
  {code}`params[0]` will be the operand (`x`), {code}`params[1]` will be a
  {code}`Tuple` containing the indices (`2, 3`), and {code}`params[2]` will
  contain the right hand side of the assignment (`y`).

classes.name: synr.ast.Slice
documentation:
A slice in an expression.

A slice in a range from \[start,end) with step size step.

# Notes

If not defined, start is 0, end is -1 and step is 1.

# Example

{code}`1:2` in {code}`x = y[1:2]`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Lambda
documentation:
A lambda expression

# Example

In {code}`lambda x, y: x + y`, {code}`x, y` are {code}`params`,
{code}`x + y` is {code}`body`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Return
documentation:
A return statement.

# Example

{code}`return x`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Assign
documentation:
An assignment statement.

# Notes

Augmented assignment statements like {code}`x += 2` are translated into an
operator call and an assignment (i.e. `x = x + 2`).

# Example

In {code}`x: int = 2`, {code}`x` is {code}`lhs`, {code}`int` is {code}`ty`,
and {code}`2` is {code}`rhs`.

classes.name: synr.ast.UnassignedCall
documentation:
A standalone function call.

# Example

```python
def my_function():
    test_call()
    return 2
```

Here {code}`test_call()` is an {code}`UnassignedCall`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Nonlocal
documentation:
A nonlocal statement.

# Example

```python x, y = 1, 2 def foo():     nonlocal x, y     return x

```

In {code}`nonlocal x, y`, {code}`vars` is :code\`[x, y]\`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Global
documentation:
A global statement.

# Example

```python x, y = 1, 2 def foo():     global x, y     return x

```

In {code}`global x, y`, {code}`vars` is :code\`[x, y]\`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Block
documentation:
A sequence of statements.

# Examples

```python
def my_function():
    test_call()
    return 2
```

Here {code}`test_call()` and {code}`return 2` forms a {code}`Block`.

```python
if x > 2:
    y = 2
```

Here {code}`y = 2` is a {code}`Block`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Function
documentation:
A function declaration.

# Example

```python
@F
def my_function(x: int):
    return x + 2
```

Here {code}`name` is {code}`my_function`, {code}`x: int` is
{code}`params[0]`, {code}`body` is {code}`return x + 2`, and
{code}`decorators` is {code}`[F]`.

classes.name: synr.ast.For
documentation:
A for statement.

# Example

```python
for x in range(2):
    pass
```

Here {code}`lhs` will be {code}`x`, {code}`rhs` will be {code}`range(2)`,
and {code}`body` will be {code}`pass`.

classes.name: synr.ast.While
documentation:
An while statement.

# Examples

```python
while x <= 2:
    pass
```

Here {code}`condition` is {code}`x <= 2`, and {code}`body` will be {code}`pass`.

classes.name: synr.ast.With
documentation:
A with statement.

# Example

```python
with open(x) as f:
    pass
```

Here {code}`lhs` will be {code}`f`, {code}`rhs` will be {code}`open(x)`,
and {code}`body` will be {code}`pass`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Assert
documentation:
An assert statement.

# Example

In {code}`assert 2 == 2, "Oh no!"`, {code}`condition` is {code}`2 == 2`,
and {code}`msg` is {code}`"Oh no!"`.

classes.name: synr.ast.If
documentation:
An if statement.

# Notes

An if statement with {code}`elif` branches becomes multiple nested if
statements.

# Examples

```python
if x == 2:
    return x
else:
    return 3
```

Here {code}`condition` is {code}`x == 2`, {code}`true` is {code}`return x`,
and {code}`false` is {code}`return 3`.

Multiple {code}`elif` statements become nested ifs. For example,

```python
if x == 2:
    return x
elif x == 3:
    return "hi"
else:
    return 3
```

becomes {code}`If('x == 2', 'return x', If('x == 3', 'return "hi"', 'return 3'))`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Class
documentation:
A class definition.

# Example

```python
class MyClass:
    x = 2
    def return_x():
        return x
```

Here {code}`name` is {code}`MyClass`, {code}`funcs["return_x"]` is
{code}`def return_x():
return x`, and {code}`assignments[0]` is
{code}`x = 2`.

classes.name: synr.ast.Module
documentation: A collection of classes and functions.
